title: Enforce skill security audit as hard architectural gate, not prompt rule
description: Currently, the skill security audit (CLAUDE.md Rule #4) is a prompt-level instruction — it relies on the model remembering to call `soul_manager.audit_skill()`. The reading note's insight is that prompt-level rules are like local demonstrations: they have near-zero weight against the model's general training distribution. Safety-critical behaviors need to be architectural gates in code, not hoped-for model compliance. Change `soul_manager.py` so that the `save_skill()` / `register_skill()` entry point unconditionally calls `audit_skill()` and raises/returns an error if audit fails — making it impossible to save a skill without passing the audit, regardless of what the model was prompted to do.
rationale: The Waymo note observes that AV 'learning' is dominated by training distribution, not local demonstrations — a few in-context examples approach zero weight. Prompt rules are the agent equivalent of local demonstrations. The note asks: can safety-critical behaviors be injected in a way that bypasses distribution weights? For code-executing agents, the answer is yes: move the check from the prompt into the call graph so it is structurally unavoidable. This is the AI agent equivalent of the 'high-priority rule injection' mechanism the note is asking about.
diff_description: In `soul_manager.py`, find the function that persists a new skill to disk (likely `save_skill`, `add_skill`, or similar). At the top of that function, before any write operation, insert: `audit_result = self.audit_skill(skill_code)` followed by a guard `if not audit_result.passed: raise SkillSecurityError(audit_result.reason)`. This makes the audit a precondition enforced by the call graph rather than a prompt instruction. No new logic is needed — `audit_skill()` already exists per the HARD RULES description. The change is purely structural: move the call from 'model decides when to call it' to 'code always calls it on every save path'.
